coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials have increasingly gained the attention of the nano-, photo- and biomedical communities owing to their unique photophysical properties. Here, we facilely synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a one-pot solvothermal reaction, and demonstrated their utility as photosensitizers for in vitro antibacterial photodynamic inactivation (aPDI). The bottom-up synthesis employed inexpensive and sustainable starting materials (citric acid), used ethanol as an environmentally-friendly solvent, was relatively energy efficient, produced minimal waste, and purification was accomplished simply by filtration. The CQDs were characterized by both physical (TEM, X-ray diffraction) and spectroscopic (UV–visible, fluorescence, and ATR-FTIR) methods, which together confirmed their nanoscale dimensions and photophysical properties. aPDI studies demonstrated detection limit inactivation (99.9999 + %) of Gram-negative Escherichia coli 8099 and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus ATCC-6538 upon visible light illumination (λ ≥ 420 nm, 65 ± 5 mW/cm2; 60 min). Post-illumination SEM images of the bacteria incubated with the CQDs showed perforated and fragmented cell membranes consistent with damage from reactive oxygen species (ROS), and mechanistic studies revealed that the bacteria were inactivated by singlet oxygen, with no discernable roles for other ROS (e.g., superoxide or hydroxyl radicals). These findings demonstrated that CQDs can be facilely prepared, operate via a Type II mechanism, and are effective photosensitizers for in vitro aPDI.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene via a one-pot solvothermal reaction, as well as their appearance as an aqueous suspension under visible and UV light illumination (365 nm).

Characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). A) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy image; inset: higher magnification of panel A depicting the interlayer spacing. B) Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) maps identifying the spatial distributions of C (red), N (magenta), and O (green), and their superposition (C/N/O). C) Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. D) UV–visible (black) and fluorescence (red) spectra (λex = 320 nm) of an aqueous solution of CQDs. E) Fluorescence spectra of the sample from panel D acquired at the excitation wavelengths indicated in the figure legend, as well as a photographic image of the aqueous CQDs under UV-illumination (λex = 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Concentration and illumination time dependent photodynamic inactivation studies employing CQDs against A) S. aureus ATCC-6538 and B) E. coli 8099. For both panels, displayed are the results for the CQDs dark control (black ◆; 1 mg/mL) and illuminated studies (Xe lamp, 500 W, λ ≥ 420 nm, 12 cm sample distance) at concentrations of 0.25 (red •), 0.5 (blue ▲), and 1 (green ■) mg/mL CQDs, all computed as the % survival versus the photosensitizer-free dark control. For graphing purposes, data points (i.e., 0% survival) that fell below the detection limit (denoted by the shaded area) were set to 0.0001%. Error bars denote ± std. dev. and are present for all data points, but may be smaller in size than the marker itself. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Scanning electron microscopy images of E. coli (A-C) and S. aureus (D-F). Panels A and D depict the untreated cells, panels B and E are the cells after incubation with the CQDs but without illumination, and panels C and F show the oxidative damage of the cells after photodynamic inactivation (1 mg/mL CQDs; see experimental for illumination conditions).

A) UV–visible spectroscopic monitoring of the photooxidation of 10 μM 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) to colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene in the presence of 35 μg/mL CQDs as a function of illumination time (532 nm laser, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2). B) The single wavelength (410 nm) trace from panel A (black), in comparison to the study performed without light (dark control; red), or without CQDs (PS-free light control; blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials have increasingly gained the attention of the nano-, photo- and biomedical communities owing to their unique photophysical properties. Here, we facilely synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a one-pot solvothermal reaction, and demonstrated their utility as photosensitizers for in vitro antibacterial photodynamic inactivation (aPDI). The bottom-up synthesis employed inexpensive and sustainable starting materials (citric acid), used ethanol as an environmentally-friendly solvent, was relatively energy efficient, produced minimal waste, and purification was accomplished simply by filtration. The CQDs were characterized by both physical (TEM, X-ray diffraction) and spectroscopic (UV–visible, fluorescence, and ATR-FTIR) methods, which together confirmed their nanoscale dimensions and photophysical properties. aPDI studies demonstrated detection limit inactivation (99.9999 + %) of Gram-negative Escherichia coli 8099 and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus ATCC-6538 upon visible light illumination (λ ≥ 420 nm, 65 ± 5 mW/cm2; 60 min). Post-illumination SEM images of the bacteria incubated with the CQDs showed perforated and fragmented cell membranes consistent with damage from reactive oxygen species (ROS), and mechanistic studies revealed that the bacteria were inactivated by singlet oxygen, with no discernable roles for other ROS (e.g., superoxide or hydroxyl radicals). These findings demonstrated that CQDs can be facilely prepared, operate via a Type II mechanism, and are effective photosensitizers for in vitro aPDI.

ce:section.ce:para: Worldwide, the percentage of all deaths attributed to infectious diseases, including respiratory infections and parasitic diseases, decreased from 23% in 2000 (12.1 million) to 17% in 2012 (9.5 million) [1,2]. Despite this improvement over a relatively short period of time, infectious diseases persist as one of the leading causes of illness and death, not just in developing nations, but across the globe: it is estimated that drug-resistant pathogens cause an annual death toll of 23,000 in the United States [3], 33,000 in the European Union [4], and a staggering 700,000 lives lost worldwide [5]. More concerning, antimicrobial resistance, i.e., the development and spread of drug-resistant pathogens that increasingly threatens our ability to treat common infections, has risen dramatically [6–8]. By some accounts, the “post-antibiotic era” has already begun [9], and that over the next 30 years the number of deaths due to drug-resistant pathogens will climb to 10 million each year, and a cumulative economic impact of 100 trillion USD [5,9]. In light of these dire predictions, new approaches for the prevention and treatment of infectious diseases are paramount.

One such approach that is being explored for preventing the transmission of various types of infections from surfaces to new hosts is to employ self-disinfecting materials based upon antimicrobial photodynamic inactivation (aPDI) [10]. Antimicrobial PDI employs a non-toxic photosensitizer (PS) with visible light illumination to generate microbicidal reactive oxygen species (ROS) from ambient molecular oxygen [11]. The ROS form via one of two mechanisms [12]: a Type I mechanism that produces hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide (O2 •–), or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and/or a Type II mechanism that produces singlet oxygen (1O2). As these ROS react with cell or viral components in a non-specific fashion, they result in substantial oxidative damage, and are thus able to inactivate a broad spectrum of microbes, from bacteria, viruses, and fungi, to even parasites [13–18]. Microbial inactivation by ROS-mediated oxidative damage has two significant advantages: i) microbes that are resistant to conventional antibiotics exhibit virtually identical levels of inactivation as their drug-susceptible counterparts [19,20], and ii) akin to bleach, the non-specific damage caused by these ROS means that the development of resistance to aPDI is highly improbably [21–24]. Together, these advantages make self-disinfecting materials based upon aPDI particularly attractive for preventing the transmission of drug-resistant pathogens from contaminated or improperly disinfected surfaces to new hosts.

Although materials based upon aPDI have shown promise for preventing the transmission of pathogenic infections, limitations do exist. Common photosensitizers [18], including natural and synthetic tetrapyrroles [25–27], phthalocyanines [28], phenothiazines [29,30] (methylene blue) and xanthene dyes [31,32] (rose Bengal), have been utilized in antimicrobial photodynamic inactivation [33], anti-cancer photodynamic therapy (PDT) [34,35], and in imaging [36]. However, many of these classical PSs have a number of drawbacks, including low water solubility/dispersibility, poor or undesirable photophysical properties, require complex synthetic routes or are difficult to purify, and/or lack biocompatibility/biodegradability, which all together limit their applications in photomedicine. To overcome these drawbacks [37], efforts have included synthetic modifications [26,38–42], conjugation to water-soluble targeting agents (e.g., small molecules, polymers, or peptides) [43–45], and the use of carriers or encapsulation methods [46–50], to name a few. More recently, the rapid development of nanotechnology has led to nanoscale materials that have garnered significant attention for therapeutic applications (e.g., biological imaging, photodynamic therapy and nanomedicine), and have included quantum dots, doped nanoparticles, and polymer dots [37,51,52].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have increasingly garnered attention as a promising nanomaterial [53–63] with a number of advantageous properties as potential photosensitizers for materials that operate via a photodynamic mode of action, including ease-of-synthesis, favorable dispersibility in water, nontoxicity, biocompatibility (heavy-metal free), and widely controllable optical properties [64,65]. Carbon quantum dots and carbon nanostructures have been commonly used in the dye-sensitized solar cells as counter electrode due to their strong electrochemical catalyst properties [66–70]. Compared with metal-based (Cd, Pb, In) semiconductor quantum dots that are inherently toxic, CQDs are, safer, sustainable, and more biocompatible than metal-based nanomaterials for therapeutic applications [61,71]. It has been reported that CQDs are able to generate ROS for PDT applications via light absorption [60,72], and as such CQDs have been utilized widely for applications in anti-cancer photodynamic therapy [73], bio-imaging [64], nanomedicine [74], photocatalysis [75], and sensing [76,77]. Compared with more traditional PSs, CQDs do not require heavy-metals, and have the advantage of being environmentally friendly. Recent studies have identified various methods for the preparation of CQDs [73,78,79], including top-down and bottom-up approaches: the former involves the breakdown of larger precursors, such as graphite powder or carbon nanotubes [80,81], whereas the latter includes the carbonization of organic precursors [82]. The bottom-up approach enables tuning of the physicochemical properties of the CQDs, such as their hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, particle size, optical properties, and surface functional groups, through judicious selection of the molecular precursors and synthetic steps [73,82]. In light of these recent advances, a number of questions have emerged regarding the scope and utility of CQDs as potential photosensitizers for their application in in vitro antimicrobial photodynamic inactivation. Are CQDs effective against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, or is one classification more susceptible than the other? If specific inactivation of a bacterium is observed, does this originate from the nanoscale dimensions of the CQDs, as opposed to the molecular scale of more traditional photosensitizers? Finally, if the CQDs are shown to be effective photosensitizers for aPDI, by which mechanism do they operate, Type I or Type II? Addressing these questions is a necessary first step if CQDs are to be incorporated into scaffolds for self-disinfecting materials.

To address these and other questions, here we synthesized CQDs from citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene in ethanol solvent using a one-pot solvothermal method (Fig. 1 ), and broadly assessed their utility as photosensitizers for the in vitro antibacterial photodynamic inactivation of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Our experimental design was inspired by a recent report of CQD synthesis by Yuan and co-workers [83], and takes advantage of the solvothermal method as a bottom-up approach to synthesize CQDs using environmentally-friendly precursors and solvents, leading to a high purity product that was characterized by a number of physical and spectroscopic methods. Furthermore, mechanistic studies were also performed to determine which reactive oxygen species were responsible for pathogen inactivation. Our findings suggest that in addition to being photosensitizers for in vitro aPDI, CQDs can be produced at a low cost from green methods, require no chromatographic purification, and represent a next generation, scalable photosensitizer for use as the basis for potent antimicrobial nanomaterials to combat the transmission of pathogens that lead to healthcare associated infections.

Analytical grade chemicals were used as received without further purification: 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (1,5-DAN), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), rose Bengal (RB), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), tri(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA), and coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA) were obtained from Shanghai Vita Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, methanol, aqueous glutaraldehyde (25%), citric acid (CA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), potassium chloride, sodium chloride, yeast extract, plate count agar, disodium hydrogen phosphate, monopotassium phosphate, tryptone, soy peptone, and Tween 80 were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All studies utilized deionized water.

Modified from a literature protocol [83], the CQDs were synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal procedure employing a mass ratio of citric acid (CA) to 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (1,5-DAN) of 1:2. Briefly, CA (130 mg, 0.68 mmol) and 1,5-DAN (260 mg, 1.64 mmol) were added to absolute ethanol (130 mL). After sonication for 10 min, the reaction mixture was placed into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (200 mL), heated for 9 h to 200 °C, and subsequently cooled to ambient temperature. The solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter, and the filtrate was freeze-dried to obtain 143 mg of the CQDs as a dark brown solid.

The morphology of the CQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM - 2100, Japan). A CQD aqueous solution was drop-cast on a carbon support film (300 mesh, Beijing Zhongjingkeyi Technology Co., Ltd), and then subjected to TEM imaging. UV–visible absorbance spectra of the CQDs were recorded on a Shimadzu spectrophotometer (UV-2600) with a wavelength range of 220–360 nm. Fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelengths indicated in the corresponding figure legend were obtained on a Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected on a Bruker D2 PHASER diffractometer with a zero background sample holder. Elemental composition and elemental mapping of the CQDs were performed on an EDAX Octane Elect Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) instrument. Thermal imaging was accomplished with an InfReC R500 infrared camera (Nippon Avionics Co., Japan). The temperature of the CQDs in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was monitored as a function of Xenon lamp illumination time (λ ≥ 420 nm, 65 ± 5 mW/cm2 fluence rate). The zeta potential of an aqueous CQD solution was measured with a Malvern Panalytical Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Bacteria were visualized on a Hitachi SU1510 scanning electron microscope. The bacteria were first centrifuged (6820 ×g), with the resultant cell pellet being resuspended in PBS solution (1 mL) containing 2.5% glutaraldehyde, and then transferred to a 24-well plate containing a glass slide (10 × 10 mm). The solution was subsequently incubated for 12 h at 5 °C to fix the morphology of bacteria, after which they were dehydrated for 10 min each via an ethanol series of 25%, 50%, 75%, 95% and 100% (v/v). Following air-drying in the dark, the cells were gold sputtered prior to visualization by SEM (5.0 kV; 10,000× and 15,000× magnification).

Escherichia coli 8099 (lysogeny broth, LB), Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 6538 (tryptic soy broth, TSB), Klebsiella pneumoniae ATCC 2146 (KP; BD Difco Nutrient Broth #234000 supplemented with 100 μg/mL ampicillin) and Acinetobacter baumannii ATCC 1605 (multi-drug resistant/MDRAB; LB-Miller media with 5 μg/mL tetracycline) were cultured overnight at 30 °C in an orbital shaker (100–120 rpm). Optical density measurements at 600 nm (OD600) were employed to culture the bacteria to an initial concentration of 1–5 × 108 colony forming units (CFU)/mL, followed by pelleting of the bacteria by centrifugation for 10 min (6820 ×g for E. coli and S. aureus, 1174 ×g for KP and MDRAB), and then they were resuspended into phosphate buffered saline (PBS) containing 0.05% Tween-80 prior to the aPDI assay.

Illumination fluence rates (mW/cm2) were measured with an Ophir Orion power meter. For aPDI studies of E. coli 8099 and S. aureus ATCC 6538, illumination was provided by a Xenon arc lamp (500 W), with a sample-to-lamp distance of 12 cm (yielding a fluence of 65 ± 5 mW/cm2), and equipped with a long-pass filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). For studies against KP and MDRAB, a LC122 PDT light (non-coherent; LumaCare, USA) was used. The LC122 was outfitted with a 400–700 nm band pass filter provided by a LUM V fiber optic probe that is capable of a ~95 ± 3% average transmittance from an OSRAM 64653 HLX Xenophot bulb (250 W, 24 V).

Triplicate aPDI studies were performed using 24-well sterile plates (flat-bottom) as previously described [84–91]. Briefly, 1 mL aliquots of a 1:1 mixture of PBS containing 1–5 × 108 CFU/mL of bacteria and CQDs solution (variable concentration prepared in sterilized ultrapure water) were pipetted into neighboring wells of identically prepared 24-well plates. One 24-well plate was illuminated per section 2.4.2 for 0–60 min, while the other was reserved in the dark (as a dark control) for the same time period. Additional wells containing only bacteria served as the PS-free (cells only) dark control. After illumination, 40 μL aliquots from each of the dark control, illuminated, and PS-free control samples were then diluted 10-fold into 360 μL PBS. Each sample was six-times (6×) serially diluted 10-fold. Each dilution series was plated (TSB-agar for S. aureus; LB-agar for E. coli; BD Difco Nutrient Broth #234000-agar for KP; LB-agar for MDRAB) as described by Jett et al. using gridded plates [92], and incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 15–24 h to enable colony counting. Cell survival was defined as the ratio of the colony counts (in CFU/mL) of the illuminated sample to that of the PS-free dark control. Using this plating method and the initial bacteria concentration of 108 CFU/mL, the highest detectable level of inactivation (i.e., the detection limit) was 6 log units (0.0001%). The data were evaluated for statistical significance via the unpaired Student's two-tailed t-test.

The ability of the CQDs to generate 1O2 upon visible light illumination was determined by the oxidative bleaching of the known 1O2 quencher 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) [93]. Briefly, a 3 mL methanol solution containing 10 μM DPBF and 35 μg/mL CQDs was placed in a cuvette, illuminated by a handheld laser (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2; HTPOW Laser Limited) for 20 min, and the UV–visible spectrum was recorded periodically with the absorbance at 410 nm used to calculate the rate of DPBF oxidation.

Adapted from a literature protocol [94], a horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-based fluorescence assay was employed to determine if the CQDs generated H2O2 upon illumination. Briefly, 5 mL of an aqueous solution containing 70 μg/mL CQDs was illuminated by a handheld laser (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2; HTPOW Laser Limited) for 20 min. Afterwards, the solution was kept in the dark and assayed every 5 min as follows: a 0.2 mL aliquot was diluted with 0.2 mL 0.1 M Tris buffer (pH 8.8) containing 1.05 mM 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA), 0.04 mg/mL HRP, and 0.6 mL deionized water. The assay mixture was reacted for 10 min, and its fluorescence spectrum was then recorded (λex = 316 nm, λem = 450 nm).

To determine if the CQDs generated O2 •– upon illumination, nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), a chemical probe that forms water-insoluble diformazan upon reaction with O2 •–, was employed per literature protocol [95]. Briefly, 3 mL of an aqueous solution containing 196 μM NBT and 1 mg/mL CQDs was transferred to a cuvette, illuminated by a handheld laser (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2; HTPOW Laser Limited) for 10 min, and its UV–visible spectrum was recorded periodically and monitored for diformazan formation at 259 nm.

Coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA), a chemical probe that generates fluorescent 7-hydroxy-coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (7OH-CCA) upon reaction with hydroxyl radicals [96], was used to determine if the CQDs generated •OH upon illumination, as follows: a 5 mL aqueous solution containing 100 μM CCA, 10 mM sodium hydroxide, and 70 μg/mL CQDs was mixed vigorously in a beaker, and illuminated by a handheld laser (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2; HTPOW Laser Limited) for 20 min. A 0.2 mL aliquot of the solution was periodically withdrawn, diluted with 0.8 mL deionized water, and its fluorescence spectrum was recorded (λex = 340 nm, λem = 450 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized from citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene via a one-pot solvothermal method (Fig. 1). Compared with other bottom-up CQD synthetic routes, our experimental design here makes use of ethanol as an environmentally-friendly solvent, employed inexpensive starting materials, was relatively energy efficient, and produced minimal waste. After the single synthetic step of carbonization in a standard laboratory autoclave, the harvested CQDs were purified simply by a filtration. As can be seen in Fig. 1, the CQDs were well-dispersed in aqueous, and exhibited a strong blue fluorescence upon UV illumination (365 nm). The morphology of the CQDs was imaged by transmission electron microscopy: the quantum dots were spherical in appearance (⌀avg = 9.6 ± 1.2 nm; Fig. S1), and exhibited clear lattice fringes with an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm (Fig. 2A) that represents the π–π stacking distance between the two layers [82,97,98]. Characteristic of a nano-sized material, the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) revealed a single broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 26° (Fig. 2C) that corresponded to the crystal lattice distance of (002) (d002 = 0.34 nm), and demonstrated a well-ordered lamellar structure with layered regularity, in accordance with previous studies of CQDs [97,98]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed to visualize the elemental distribution in the CQDs: as expected, carbon (81.9 wt%), nitrogen (13.8 wt%) and oxygen (4.3 wt%) were uniformly distributed throughout the sample (Fig. 2B). The electronic absorption spectrum of the CQDs exhibited a strong feature at 230 nm and a smaller, more broad absorption at 310–330 nm (Fig. 2D), which are consistent with literature reports ascribing these features respectively to π-π* (C=C) and n-π* (C=O) transitions [82,99,100]. The CQD optical properties were further interrogated with photoluminescence spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths (280–360 nm). The CQDs exhibited an emission maximum at 404 nm, and a maximal fluorescence intensity was achieved using an excitation wavelength (λex) of 320 nm (Fig. 2E). Moreover, the emission maximum did not vary as a function of excitation wavelength, which is indicative of a uniform size and surface properties for the CQDs [101,102]. Finally, the surface charge (zeta potential) of the CQDs was found to be +44 ± 1.6 mV. The observed positive potential is likely due to the presence of positively charged amino groups as demonstrated by the ATR-FTIR spectroscopic characterization of the CQDs. Specifically, stretching vibration bands corresponding to OH, NH, CC, CN, and CN were observed at 3373, 3247, 1630, 1519, 1373 cm−1, respectively [103,104], with a peak at 1584 cm−1 attributed to NH bending (Fig. S2) [105].

in vitro antimicrobial photodynamic inactivation (aPDI) studies of the synthesized CQDs as photosensitizers were performed in both concentration and time dependent manners against the model bacteria S. aureus ATCC-6538 (Gram-positive) and E. coli 8099 (Gram-negative) (Fig. 3 ). The assays were performed with a fixed illumination intensity of 65 ± 5 mW/cm2 for 0–60 min, with an initial starting concentration of bacteria in the range of 1–5 × 108 CFU/mL. S. aureus ATCC-6538 (Gram-positive) was found to be susceptible to photodynamic inactivation by the CQDs: at a concentration of 1 mg/mL with 60 min illumination, the CQDs inactivated S. aureus to the detect limit of 6 log units (99.9999 + % viable cell eradiation; P < 0.0001; Fig. 3A). At lower photosensitizer concentrations, inactivation was also observed, but to a lesser degree: 0.5 mg/mL CQDs inactivated S. aureus by 99.7% (2.7 log units; P = 0.00027) after 60 min illumination, while 0.25 mg/mL CQDs did not show more than a 2.6-fold decrease in viable cells. No significant inactivation was seen at shorter illumination times (e.g., 30 min), which highlights the greater relative importance of illumination time vs. PS concentration in these studies: a 2-fold change in total fluence at constant PS concentration (1 mg/mL CQDs) yielded a ~6 log units difference in pathogen inactivation, whereas a 2-fold change in PS concentration while holding the illumination constant (60 min) led to a 2–4 log units variation in inactivation. In the presence of the quantum dot photosensitizer but absence of light (dark control), no statistically significant inactivation was seen, which both confirms a photodynamic antibacterial effect and demonstrates a lack of dark toxicity.

Gram-negative E. coli 8099 was found to be highly photoinactivated by the CQDs: at all concentrations (0.25–1 mg/mL), the CQDs achieved the detection limit inactivation of the pathogen by 99.9999 + % (6 log units; P < 0.0001; Fig. 3A) at 60 min illumination. However, similar to the results obtained for S. aureus, a 2-fold decrease in illumination time to 30 min led to a marked decrease in inactivation, achieving only 1 log unit reduction at the highest PS concentration of 1 mg/mL. Highlighting the bacterium-specific responses of the CQDs to mediate bacterial photodynamic inactivation, aPDI studies employing the Gram-negative bacterium K. pneumoniae ATCC 2146 (KP) and multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii ATCC 1605 (MDRAB) with 60 min light illumination showed more modest results, with 89.3% (0.95 log units; P = 0.011) reduction in KP at 5 mg/mL, and 93.1% (1.5 log units; P = 0.00013) pathogen inactivation for MDRAB at 1 mg/mL (Fig. S3). The lower efficacies of the CQDs in mediating the photodynamic inactivation of KP and MDRAB were expected, as our previous studies employing organic photosensitizers in solution (TMPyP, methylene blue, and DIMPy-BODIPY) [13] or embedded/covalently attached to materials (Por(+)-Paper [106], PAN-Por(+) [107], ZnTMPyP4+/OBC26 [88]) have consistently demonstrated that they are less susceptible to photodynamic inactivation than the other bacteria studied here. Given that these results appear to be independent of the type of photosensitizer (molecular vs. quantum dot) employed, we surmise that the higher tolerance of KP and MDRAB to photodynamic inactivation are due to type-specific reasons (e.g., cell wall thickness, composition). As was found for S. aureus, the dark controls revealed no statistically significant inactivation of these Gram-negative bacteria when the studies were performed in the absence of illumination. For all bacteria, PS-free light controls have also previously shown no statistically-significant effect under the illumination conditions examined here, ruling out an antimicrobial effect by light alone [13].

Although it is widely acknowledged that the presence of the additional lipopolysaccharide outer cell membrane of a Gram-negative bacterium renders its photodynamic inactivation comparatively less effective than against a Gram-positive bacterium [33], here we found that E. coli was more easily inactivated by the CQDs than S. aureus, a finding that is consistent with previous aPDI studies employing Mn-doped Zn/S quantum dots [108]. As one possible explanation for the differential activity observed here between E. coli and S. aureus, we suggest that the physical interaction between the CQDs and the bacteria is a contributing factor in modulating the effectiveness of photodynamic inactivation; this physical interaction should be viewed in light of the diffusion of singlet oxygen in solution being <200 nm in water [109,110], which localizes the microbicidal effect to within a fraction of the size of a bacterium (~1 μm) from the surface of the quantum dot. Here, a comparison of the zeta potentials suggests that the CQDs (+44 mV) favor a stronger electrostatic interaction between the PS and E. coli (−49 mV [111]) vs. S. aureus (−31.7 mV [111]), leading to higher inactivation of E. coli through enhanced PS/bacterium interactions. While this suggests that the surface properties of the quantum dots may play an important role in photodynamic inactivation, another factor may be the size of the CQDs versus the morphology of the bacteria themselves: S. aureus tends to form grape-like clusters (vide infra) [112], and it is possible that some bacteria residing within the interior of these clusters could be protected from damage given the aforementioned limited diffusibility of ROS (primarily 1O2), as well as the inability of the larger nanoscale CQD-based PS to penetrate into the interior of these clusters compared to the sub-nanoscale of traditional, molecular-sized PS that are more diffusible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning Electron Microscopy of Inactivated Bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: To further study how photodynamic inactivation impacts E. coli and S. aureus, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to image the bacteria in the presence of the CQDs, both prior to and post illumination (Fig. 4 ). In the absence of the CQDs, the bacteria exhibited typical sizes and morphologies: E. coli appeared as a rod-shaped bacterium, whereas S. aureus have a spherical appearance and resembled grape-like clusters, and both presented with smooth, intact surfaces (Fig. 4A and D). When incubated with the CQDs prior to illumination, the morphologies were same as the native bacterial cells, and no surface damage could be observed (Fig. 4B and E). Post-illumination (Xe lamp, 500 W, 12 cm sample distance, λ ≥ 420 nm; 1 h), however, significant damage to the cellular structure of both bacteria was noted (Fig. 4C and F), including surface deformation, volume loss, and/or a flattening of the cells, all of which strongly suggest cell membrane damage and subsequent leakage [113]. These findings are in agreement with a previous study that showed significant oxidative damage of S. aureus by the photodynamic material RC-TETA-PPIX-Zn [89], a regenerated cellulose scaffold that contains a covalently appended porphyrin-based photosensitizer. Consistent with our above supposition that S. aureus was less susceptible that E. coli to photodynamic inactivation by the CQDs, intact S. aureus was still observable in Fig. 4F, and suggests that the nanosized CQDs are less able to penetrate into the interior of such clusters compared to traditional molecular-scale PS. Overall, the SEM images are consistent with bacterial cell damage by ROS that were generated photodynamically upon illumination of the CQDs, and motivated us to explore which ROS was specifically responsible for the inactivation of these bacteria (vide infra).

To further investigate the mechanism(s) of antibacterial photodynamic inactivation by the CQDs, we explored if four types of ROS were generated upon their illumination: singlet oxygen (1O2) via a Type II mechanism, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide (O2 •–), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) that are formed by a Type I mechanism.

As a colorimetric chemical trapping agent, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) is known to react with singlet oxygen with high specificity, undergoing oxidative bleaching to form colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene [93]. The absorbance feature attributed to DPBF at 410 nm progressively decreased in the presence of the CQDs under a 532 nm laser illumination (Fig. 5 ). When the studies were performed in pure methanol (PS-free control) or without illumination (PS-present dark control), no obvious decrease in DPBF absorption was noted (Fig. S4).

The oxidation of 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA) by H2O2 as catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase yields fluorescent dimers (λem = 450 nm) that can be used as a reporter assay for hydrogen peroxide production in solution [94]. When performed in the presence of the CQD photosensitizer under illumination (Fig. S5), no meaningful changes in fluorescence intensity at 450 nm were observed; minor signal fluctuations were noted, however these were attributable solely to the fluorescence of the CQDs (404 nm emission, see Fig. 2E). As such, we rule out the photodynamic formation of H2O2 upon illumination of the CQDs.

Nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT; λmax = 259 nm) has been shown to be reduced by O2 •– leading to water-insoluble diformazan [95]. However, as shown in Fig. S6, no changes were observed when NBT was illuminated in the presence or absence of the CQD photosensitizer, indicating that O2 •– was not photodynamically generated.

Hydroxyl radicals oxidize coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA) to fluorescent 7-hydroxy-coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (7OH-CCA) [96]. When added to a solution of CQDs under illumination (Fig. S7), no meaningful changes to the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm were observed. Similar to the HRP-assay above (Fig. S5), minor signal fluctuations attributable solely to the fluorescence of the CQDs (404 nm emission) were noted, and we conclude that the CQD photosensitizer did not generate •OH via a photodynamic mode of action.

As an alternative to ROS formation, we explored if the CQDs could function via a photothermal [114] mechanism for bacterial inactivation. The time-dependent temperature elevation of the CQDs in PBS (1 mg/mL) upon visible light illumination (λ ≥ 420 nm, 65 ± 5 mW/cm2 fluence rate) was determined by thermal imaging (Fig. S8) and compared to a PS-free control (PBS only). Both showed an identical temperature increase by ~11.8 °C after 60 min illumination, confirming that the CQDs lack a photothermal capability. Note that the increase in temperature to ~30 °C is at or below the temperature employed for bacteria cell culturing, and therefore simple thermal (infrared) heating is also ruled out as the basis for the inactivation demonstrated above.

In summary, the data are consistent with the generation of biocidal singlet oxygen (1O2) by CQDs upon visible light illumination, and establishes the mechanism of antibacterial photodynamic inactivation by the CQDs as proceeding through a Type II pathway.

In conclusion, carbon quantum dots were facilely synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal reaction, and their ability to function as photosensitizers in the antibacterial photodynamic inactivation of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria in vitro was demonstrated. While inactivation of multi-drug resistant A. baumannii and NDM-1 positive K. pneumoniae was modest, the CQDs exhibited detection limit killing of S. aureus and E. coli at PS-concentrations as low as 1 mg/mL and 0.25 mg/mL, respectively. As a Type-II photosensitizer, photodynamic inactivation by the CQDs was attributed to singlet oxygen production, and the higher activity against E. coli compared to S. aureus (as represented by a lower PS concentration needed to achieve the same level of inactivation) was linked to two factors: a stronger electrostatic interaction between E. coli and the CQDs as revealed by zeta potential measurements that favors PS-bacteria association, and the propensity for S. aureus to form clusters that likely limits PS-bacteria interaction, with this latter point being specific to nano-sized PS (e.g., CQDs) that are likely unable to diffuse into the interior of such a cluster when compared to a molecular PS (such as methylene blue). When viewed alongside our recent study employing Mn-doped Zn/S quantum dots that exhibited excellent activity against MDRAB and KP, but comparatively poor activity against S. aureus, the complementarity of the CQDs suggests that a combination of both quantum dot-based PS (i.e., CQDs + Zn/S QDs) could provide a broader antibacterial effect than achievable by either PS alone. Future studies that employ such a combination approach, as well as the incorporation of CQDs into materials, are planned to further demonstrate the promise of CQDs as novel PS for photodynamic materials to prevent pathogen transmission from surfaces to new hosts.
